front: What is Just In Time compilation? (JIT)
back: Compilation during runtime. Combines the speed of compiled code with the flexibility of interpretation.

front: What is the downside of JIT?
back: There is a delay in the initial execution

front: What is Static Analysis?
back: Analysis of the source code without executing the program

front: When is a function computable?
back: When there exists a program which computes it.

front: Which is used more in practice, compilation or interpretation?
What is partial evaluation?
back: A partial evaluator is like a across between an interpreter and a compiler. The partial evaluator will take input and only evaluate the parts of the program the depend on those inputs.

front: What is an interpreter?
back: Program that interprets instructions. Fetch-decode-execute. When a language is interpreted, the program runs from the interpreter.

front: What is the difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
back: A compiler is faster, but less flexible, and an interpreter is slower but more flexible. Compilers will not compile if there is an error in the code, while an interpreter will stop if there is a code. Interpreters also don't generate executables

front: How does a partial evaluator work?
back: It reads some source code, then builds an abstract syntax tree from which it reads some of the inputs to the program and analyses the program to see when the inputs provided are used in the program.

front: What is an abstract machine?
back: An abstract machine is a set of data structures and algorithms that can store and run programs. An abstraction of a physical computer.

front: What is the name of the technique that consists of evaluating a program against an input to produce a program that is more efficient than the original
What is a compiler?
back: A compiler is a program that is used for translation to target machine code. A compiler directly translates to the machine code and generates an intermediate file, which acts as an executable that you can run.

front: What is the first step of an interpreter’s execution cycle?
back: Fetching the next instruction to execute from memory

front: When were low-level languages used to program the first computers?
What is the program that translates assembly to machine code called?
What is OpCode?
back: A unique code which identifies one of the primitive operations defined by the machine’s hardware

front: The operations for processing primitive data are implemented by what?
What was the most famous computer on which microprogramming was used?
What is the term used for simulation using microprogramming?
In real language implementations, is compilation or interpreation usually used?
back: Both elements are almost always present.

front: What kind of machine is the Java Virtual Machine and its bytecode language an example of?
What happens in the Design of Language stage?
back: Bindings between primitive constants, types and operations of the language are defined (for example, + indicates addition, and int denotes the type of integers etc.)

front: Does there exist programs which are not computable?
back: The undecidability of the halting problem assures us that there exist functions which are not computable.

front: How can a program take another program as an input and determine if it terminates?
What is the halting problem?
back: The halting problem is a problem which states that no program can be written to predict whether or not any other program halts after a finite number of steps.

front: Where is a declaration local to a block visible?
back: That block and all the blocks nested within it

front: When does the environment change?
back: On the entry and exit of a block.

front: What are Denotable Objects?
back:
Objects to which a name can be given.
Here is a non-exhaustive list: 

  • Objects whose names are defined by the user: Variables, formal parameters, procedures, user-defined types, labels, modules, user-defined constants, exceptions
  • Objects whose names are defined by the programming language: primitive types, primitive operations, predefined constants

front: When are changes in the environment produced?
back: At entry and exit from a block

front: Which programming languages are turing complete (meaning they can compute the same set of functions as those computed by a Turing Machine)?

Take the following example: (c-like)
{
    int a = 2; 
    {...}
}
To who is a visible?
back: The block it has been declared in, as well as all blocks listed within it, unless there is a new declaration of the same name in that same block.

front: If we have a variable, integer a = 10, do we know whether the name and the object are the same?
back: The name and the object they denote are never the same. No matter if it's a function or a variable or anything else.

front: What is the difference between static and lexical scope?
back: They are two words for the same thing

front: What are names?
back:
Sequence of characters used to denote something. E.g. 
int name = 2
const pi = 3.14

front: Which functionality manages the allocation of memory for programs and for data?
A stack uses which scheme, LIFO, FIFO or FILO?
What is the difference in an activation record for a procedure and for a function?
back: For functions the activation record must also keep tabs on the memory location in which the function stores its return value.

front: A block A is entered, then a block B is entered, with the LIFO scheme, which block do we leave first?
What is the LIFO scheme?
Is memory management a function of a compiler or interpreter associated with an abstract machine?
When is static memory management performed and by what?
How is the runtime management of the system stack implemented?
back: By code fragments which the compiler or interpreter inserts immediately before and after the call to a procedure or before the start and after the end of a block

front: What is the memory space allocated on the stack, dedicated to an in-line block or to an activation of a procedure called?
What does the structure of a generic activation record for an in-line block hold?
back: Dynamic chain pointer, local variables and intermediate results

front: What are intermediate results in an in-line block of an activation record?
back: Results which are only needed for something else. For example b = (a + x) / (x + y), a+x and x+y will be calculated and stored before they are divided (depending on the compiler).

front: What is a procedure?
back: An independent code module that fulfils some concrete task and is referenced within a larger body of source code

front: Where does statically allocated memory objects reside?
back: In a fixed zone of memory which is determined by the compiler, and they remain there for the entire duration of the program's execution.

front: Are constants, object code and global variables usually statically or dynamically allocated?
What does the dynamic chain pointer store? (recall: in-line block of a generic activation record)
back: A pointer to the previous activation record on the stack (or to the last activation record created).

front: What does it mean that we are using a stack for memory purposes?
back: We use them to push activation records or frames.

front: Why do we need memory?
back: To store the state of the program

front: Why do we need a stack for dynamic memory, instead of just dumping everything in memory?
back: We need it for recursion. With recursion we cannot allocate all possible blocks, but we can with a stack.

front: How do we implement static scoping?
back: We are using the static chain pointer. The compiler adds this pointer to the previous "father" block, which contains the known local variable.

front: What is an expression?
back: A syntactic entity whose evaluation yields a value or does not end, in which case the expression is undefined. There are three main notations, infix, prefix and postfix.

front: What are side effects?
back: Changing the state of computation without returning a value

front: When was if then else introduced?
Why do we not use goto anymore?
back: It makes the code harder to read

front: What kinds of data structures are we using in memory management ?
What is call by result?
back: Opposite of value. The value of the result is returned and modified, but is not a reference. foo(result y) {y + 1}, then y would be y + 1 from here on.

front: Why do we care about tail recursion?
What happens to the variable inside of a function using call by result after the function is done running?
Why do we care about expressions?
What are the two main ways that parameters are passed?
You can call by value and by reference, but what else?
Can you do lazy evaluation if you evaluate right-to-left?
What is type equivalence by name?
back: Two types are equivalent if they have the same name. Used in Pascal, Ada and Java

front: Type LessThan = 0..9 is an example of which type?
What is a data type?
back: A collection of values (homogeneous and effectively represented (can be visualized)) equipped with a set of operations to manipulate these values.

front: What defines what is a type and what is not a type?
When were enumerations introduced?
What is a scalar type?
back: A type where values are not composed of aggregations of other values (e.g. boolean, chars).

front: What are the pros of call by value?
back: You don't have to worry about side-effects.

front: How many categories of types are there?
back: Two, scalar types (or simple types) and composite types.

front: When are two types equivalent?
back: If each object of type T1 is also an object of type T2 and vice versa. T1 is compatible with T2 if an object of T1 can appear where a T2 would be expected.

front: When were intervals (subranges) introduced?
What is the particularity of call by constant?
back: It's read only. The implementation depends on the compiler, it can be like passing by value or reference

front: How many ways of passing parameters are there?
back: 6, Call by value, reference, result, result-value, constant and name

front: What are composite types?
back: Types obtained by combining other types with appropriate constructors (e.g. string, pointer, struct).

front: What are the pros of call by reference?
Are enumerations ordered or unordered?
What is the difference between an exception and an error?
back: An Error "indicates serious problems that a reasonable application should not try to catch." An Exception "indicates conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch."

front: What is the idea of finally?
back: To have a block that will be executed no matter what in a try block.

front: What was the first high-level programming language designed for computers? (Not implemented)
back: Plankalkül (Plan Calculus) in 1942-1945 by Konrad Zuse

front: Which programming language introduced the use of blocks?
Why is lisp significant?
back: It was the first functional programming language, as well as the only programming language for artificial intelligence for years.

front: Which was the first effectively implemented high-level programming language?
What was the first programming language?
When did Charles Babbage invent the Analytical Engine?
What are Lisps sucessors?
What is (was) the use-case of cobol?
back: Business-oriented computations. It has a very strict program organization and poor control structures.

front: Who invented the Analytical Engine?
What programming style (Imperative, Functional, Declarative, Functional Logic) is Prolog?
What is Java originally designed for?
back: Internet programming, but is a general-purpose language.

front: What did fortran introduce?
Where would Fortran still be used?
back: In engineering applications that require much array manipulation

front: What is significant about basic?
back:
It's the first programming language for personal computers. 
It's also designed to be easy to learn (however not anymore).

front: What did Simula 67 introduce?
What was the predecessor of Smalltalk and C++?
What is Pascal (1970) a sucessor to?
back: Algol60. It is conceptually simplified and cleaned up

front: What was the first functional programming language?
What is significant about Prolog (1972)?
back:
It has an important role in Artificial Intelligence. 
It is intended primarily as a declarative programming language.
Logic is expressed as relations

front: What connection does Simula 67 have to Algol 60?
back: It's an extension of Algol 60 designed for concurrent processes

front: What is the difference between C (1972) and C++ (1980)?
back: C++ is an object-oriented extension of the imperative language C. Designed in 1980.

front: What did algol60 introduce?
Why is cobol still used?
back: For maintenance of legacy business applications.

front: What is the fundamental cycle?
What is the part of the description of the language which seeks to answer the question of what does a correct phrase mean?
What is the part of a language description which asks itself “how do we use a meaningful sentence?”
Who developed techniques to describe syntactic phenomena in a formal manner?
What is a fundamental device for the description of programming languages?
What is the aim of lexical analysis?
back: to read the symbols (characters) forming the program sequentially from the input (and generate any errors if needed)

front: What is a very simple operation that scans the input text of the source program from left to right?
What do most official programming language definitions use for their semantic description?
Semantics is a relation between what?
What determines the correct sentences with which a recipe is expressed?
What does Chomsky use to limit the ambiguity that is always present in language?
What is a fundamental tool for describing the syntax of programming languages?
What are Context-Free Grammars?
back: a notation for the concise and precise expression of recursive definitions of strings

front: In the context of programming languages, context-free grammars were used for the first time in the definition of what language?
What is BNF?
Is a derivation tree ordered or unordered?
What are nodes without children?
Who introduced the Turing Machine language in the 1930s?
What is a Turing machine composed of?
Alonzo Church proposed a principle in the mid-1930s that states exactly what?
back: It states that a function on the natural numbers can be calculated by an effective method if and only if it is computable by a Turing machine. This is known as the church thesis.

front: What is a program that can determine whether a program can generate a division by zero error during execution?
What is the name of the machine that computes every intuitively computable function?
Church's Thesis is a philosophical principle that the computer science community assumes that it will not be discredited by what new computational paradigm?
What is nothing more than a sequence of characters used to represent, or denote another object?
When we define a name using a variable, we introduce a symbolic identifier for what?
If we define a variable fie and assign it the value of 2, where weill the value be stored?
back: the location reserved for the variable named fie

front: Why do we use names/what does the use of names avoid?
back: The need to bother with whatever the location is

front: How must the correspondence between name and memory location be guaranteed?
What is the part of the implementation responsible for the associations between names and objects that they denote?
What is the component of the abstract machine that allows the determination of what the correct association is?
Does the environment exist at the level of the physical machine?
What is a structuring method for programs introduced by ALGOL60?
What are the start- and end-block constructs for languages in the ALGOL family?
What are the start- and end-block constructs for Java?
A block's environment is composed of associations between names declared locally to what?
In most languages allowing blocks, what can be nested?
How can blocks be nested?
back: In most languages allowing blocks, blocks can be nested; that is, the definition of one block can be wholly included in that of another

front: What does the new declaration hide in the block that contains the redefinition?
When will the association of a variable from a block be reactivated?
back: on exit from the inner block

front: What are visibility rules also called?
“The rule of nearest nested scope” is a term used to describe which scope rule?
When was the static scope rule introduced?
What type of scope rule is typically used by the most important programming languages?
What is the scope rule that is easiest to implement?
What is a name?
back: a (possibly meaningful) sequence of characters used to represent some other thing

front: What term denotes the entire period of time between starting and termination of a program?
What is the environment formed from associations created when the program's execution began?
What happens when a block is exited?
back: associations for names declared locally to the block and the objects they denote are destroyed

front: What is a situation in which an object is possible to access an object whose memory has been reallocated?
What are some elements for which it is possible to allocate memory statically?
Does a language with only static memory allocation support recursion?
What did the first versions of FORTRAN use, static or dynamic memory allocation?
Intermediate results, local variables, and dynamic chain pointer are fields of what?
What does a function return when it terminates its execution?
Activation records are stored on and removed from the stack at what time?
What do we use to indicate the program or procedure that performs a call and the procedure that has been called?
What is the definition of a heap that we use here (in memory) has nothing to do with this data structure?
back: a heap is simply an area of memory in which blocks of memory can be allocated and deallocated relatively freely

front: What happens when a block of size strictly larger than the requested by the program is allocated?
What type of management is a Heap?
When did the first electronic computers appear?
Data and program were initially represented on what type of cards?
What was the first programming language that introduced expressions such as a * 2 + b ?
How many kinds of environments are there?
What does ALGOL stand for?
When were imperative languages introduced?
What are context-free grammars now known as?
What is the acronym for COmmon Business Oriented Language?
What was the goal of the COBOL language?
back: producing a language that was specific to commercial applications and whose syntax was as close as possible to the English language

front: What was the first language to introduce the concepts of class, object, subtype and dynamic method dispatch?
What language did the Pascal compiler translate into?
What was the first commercial PC?
Java uses what type of scope?
Java allows the dynamic loading of what?
What is the essential characteristic of an expression that differentiates it from a command?
How can expression syntax be precisely described?
What can be used to represent an expression internally inside a computer?
How many main types of notation can we distinguish?
What is another name for Postfix Notation?
In imperative programming languages, expression evaluation is what?
What are the two classes declarative languages are usually divided into?
What is another name for short-circuit evaluation?
If the result type of a function is void, what does the function return?
C and Java only allow one passing mechanism, which one?
What is the difference between formal parameters and actual parameters?
back: Formal parameters appear in the definition of a function, actual parameters appear in the call.

front: When is a parameter of input type?
back: If it allows communication which is only in the direction from the caller to the function.

front: When is a parameter of output type?
back: If it allows communication which is only in the direction from the function to the caller.

front: When is a parameter of input/output type?
back: If it allows for bidirectional communication between the caller and callee (function).

front: What are l-values?
back: l-values are those values that usually indicate locations and therefore are the values of expressions that can be on the left of an assignment command.

front: What are r-values?
back: r-values are the values that can be stored in locations, and therefore are the values of expressions that can appear on the right of an assignment command

front: What is the relation between the formal parameter and the actual parameter in call by reference?
back: The formal and the actual are two names for the same variable. Each modification of the formal parameter is a modification of the actual parameter.

front: Can you do call by reference in c?
back: No, but you can simulate it using pointers.

front: What is a higher order function?
back: A function which accepts functions as parameters, or returns a function.

front: How do you implement shallow binding?
back: Look for every names last association in the environment.

front: Which is harder to implement, shallow binding or deep binding?
What is an exception?
back: An event that is checked during the execution of a program and which must not (or cannot) be handled in the normal flow of control.

front: Deep binding requires what for implementation?
When is a type system (and, by extension, a language) type safe? (also known as strongly typed)
back: When no program can violate the distinctions between types defined in that language. Basically Type safety means that the compiler will validate types while compiling, and throw an error if you try to assign the wrong type to a variable.

front: When is a value denotable?
back: If it can be associated with a name.

front: When is a value expressible?
back: If it can be the result of a complex expression.

front: When is a value storable?
back: If it can be stored in a variable.

front: When does a program have static typing?
back: If its checking of type constraints can be conducted on the program text at compile time

front: When does a program have dynamic typing?
back: If its checking of type constraints can be conducted during runtime

front: Why are type errors undecidable?
back: If the type cannot be reached (halting problem), there is no way to find the type error.

front: What is the alternative to equivalence by name?
When is a type definition transparent?
back: When the name of the type is just an abbreviation for the expression defining the type.

front: Do most languages use type equivalence by name or structural equivalence?
back: A mix of both. Usually it depends on the type

front: When are two types compatible?
back: Type T is compatible with type S if a value of type T is permitted in any context in which a value of type S would be admissible.

front: What is type coercion?
back: If a language allows compatibility between the types T and S, it also allows a value of type T to occur where a value of type S is expected. When this happens, the compiler inserts a type conversion between T and S.

front: What are explicit conversions? (or casts)
back: Annotations in the language which specify that a value of one type must be converted to that of another type.

front: When is a type system monomorphic?
back: When any language object (value, function, etc) has a unique type.

front: When is a type system polymorphic?
back: When the same object can have more than one type.

front: When is a name overloaded? (ad hoc polymorphism)
back: When it corresponds to more than one object and context information must be used to determine which object is denoted by a specific instance of that name.

front: When does a value exhibit universal parametric polymorphism?
back: When it has an infinite number of different types which can be obtained by instantiating a single schema of general type.

front: What is explicit polymorphism?
back: Explicit annotations. For example generics in Java, or templates in C++

front: What is implicit polymorphism?
back: The programmer need not provide any type indications and instead the type checker tries to determine for each object, the most general type from which the other types can be obtained by instantiation.

front: When does a value exhibit subtype (or bounded) polymorphism?
back: When there is an infinity of different types which can be obtained by instantiating a general type scheme, substituting for a parameter the subtypes of an assigned type.

front: What is type inference?
back: the automatic detection of the type of an expression in a formal language

front: What is garbage collection?
back: The process of managing memory, automatically. It finds the unused objects (that are no longer used by the program) and delete or remove them to free up the memory.

front: What are pointers with a value other than null that point to storage that is no longer allocated?
What do modern languages tend to prefer over coercions?
What are tombstones?
back: a mechanism to detect dangling pointers and mitigate the problems they can cause in computer programs

front: Why do locks and keys cost more than tombstones?
back: because it is necessary to associate an additional word with each pointer

front: What are shadowed variables?
back: When two variables are declared with the same name in a sub- and super-class the sub-class shadows the superclass, meaning that you access the sub-class rather than the super-class.

front: What is overriding?
back: Overriding is a concept in object-orientation, where you redefine, or override, a method or variable implementation from a super class.

front: How is overriding different from shadowing?
back: Overriding is different from shadowing, in that instead of just pushing the old definition back, you redefine the current definition.

front: What is overloading?
back: With overloading you have two or more of the same types, but with different arguments. Depending on the argument, you choose which method to use.

front: What is the difference between overloading and overriding?
back: With overloading you need to have at least two definitions, while with overriding you need just one. It is also important in overriding that you have the same number of arguments.

front: What is runtime polymorphism also known as?
What is compile-time polymorphism also known as?
What does it mean that a method is virtual?
back: When a method is virtual is allows for overriding. A virtual function is also inheritable.

front: Does Java allow for virtual methods?
back: By default all non-static functions in Java are virtual.

front: What is the difference between shadowing and overriding?
back: Shadowing redefines an entire method or function. Overriding redefines only the implementation of a method or function.
